question_nl: Compare the top 5 wettest stations with the top 5 driest stations across all the islands.
notes: Station 0115 excluded due to 7999mm sensor error values inflating apparent rainfall.
intent: Contrast the extremes of Hawaiian rainfall distribution and explain the geographic/orographic patterns
scientific_context: Hawaii has extreme rainfall gradients driven by orographic effects -- trade winds produce heavy windward precipitation and leeward rain shadows. The wet/dry ratio can exceed 10x within a single island.
reasoning_sketch: 1. Compute avg daily rainfall per station with >1yr data 2. Rank wet and dry 3. Recognize station 0115 is inflated by sensor errors and exclude 4. Compare frequency (pct_rainy_days) vs intensity (avg_rainy_day_mm) to understand what drives the difference
expected_answer_shape: Table with rank, station_id, name, island, elev_m, days_with_data, avg_daily_mm, est_annual_mm, est_annual_in, max_single_day_mm, pct_rainy_days -- 5 wet + 5 dry rows
pitfalls: Station 0115 sensor errors make it appear as wettest by far -- must recognize and exclude
Frequency drives wet/dry differences more than per-event intensity
Even the driest stations can get extreme single-day rainfall
follow_up_questions: How does elevation correlate with rainfall across all stations?
Are there stations where the wet/dry pattern reverses seasonally?
How many other stations have similar 7999mm error patterns?
assumptions: Daily summary MV has sufficient temporal coverage for meaningful averages
Stations with <365 days of data are excluded
